content.text: Every GenAI service deployment that changes a data schema, vector index structure, prompt history format, or evaluation cache must have a data migration rollback plan -- not just a service rollback plan. A service rollback reverts the application code; it does not undo data changes already written to the database. If the migration has already backfilled 10 million rows and the service rollback reverts the code, the old code now reads data written by the new schema and may crash or produce corrupt output.

Data migration rollback is harder than service rollback because data is stateful. This document covers the three main patterns for backward-compatible migration with safe rollback paths, and the GenAI-specific data stores that require special treatment.

attrs.alt: Three migration strategies: Expand/Contract, Dual-Write, and Snapshot+Restore. GenAI-specific concerns include vector index re-embedding, prompt history schema, eval cache invalidation, fine-tune artifacts, and user feedback store.

content.text: The Core Problem: Service and Data Rollbacks Are Independent

When a deployment has both a service change and a data migration, rolling back the service does not roll back the data. The rollback plan must address both axes independently and specify what combinations of (service version, data state) are safe to run.
Only safe if migration is backward-compatible (expand pattern)
v2 code expects new columns -- will error

The expand/contract pattern is the only one that makes the (v1 service, v2 data) cell safe. That is its primary design goal: ensure that rolling back the service code while leaving the data migration in place does not break the old code.
If a migration is not backward-compatible with the previous service version, both must be rolled back together, and the rollback window is much smaller.
Expand/contract splits a migration into three separate deployments, each independently rollbackable.

Add the new column, table, or index without removing or modifying existing ones. For relational databases, add columns as nullable. For vector stores, add a new index alongside the existing one, not replacing it.

-- Phase 1: Expand -- add the new embedding dimension column
-- The old service does not write to this column, so it remains NULL
ALTER TABLE document_embeddings
  ADD COLUMN embedding_v2 vector(1536),
  ADD COLUMN embedding_model_v2 VARCHAR(64);

CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_embedding_v2
  ON document_embeddings
  USING ivfflat (embedding_v2 vector_cosine_ops)
  WITH (lists = 100);
After Phase 1 deploys, rollback is trivially safe: drop the new column. No data has been migrated yet and no service code depends on the new column.

Deploy the new service version that writes to both old and new columns on every write. Run a background backfill job to populate the new column for all existing rows. All reads use the old column during Phase 2 -- the service can be rolled back to Phase 1 at any point during the backfill.

# scripts/backfill_embeddings_v2.py
import psycopg2, openai, time, logging

BATCH_SIZE = 500
SLEEP_BETWEEN_BATCHES = 0.5

def run_backfill(conn_str: str) -> None:
    conn = psycopg2.connect(conn_str)
    cur = conn.cursor()

    cur.execute('SELECT COUNT(*) FROM document_embeddings WHERE embedding_v2 IS NULL')
    total = cur.fetchone()[0]

    while True:
        cur.execute(
            'SELECT id, content FROM document_embeddings WHERE embedding_v2 IS NULL ORDER BY id LIMIT %s',
            (BATCH_SIZE,)
        )
        rows = cur.fetchall()
        if not rows:
            break
        texts = [r[1] for r in rows]
        resp = openai.embeddings.create(model='text-embedding-ada-002', input=texts)
        embeddings = [e.embedding for e in resp.data]
        cur.executemany(
            'UPDATE document_embeddings SET embedding_v2 = %s::vector, embedding_model_v2 = %s WHERE id = %s',
            [(emb, 'text-embedding-ada-002', row[0]) for emb, row in zip(embeddings, rows)]
        )
        conn.commit()
        time.sleep(SLEEP_BETWEEN_BATCHES)

After 100% of rows have the new column populated and all service instances read from it, drop the old column. Phase 3 is irreversible: once the old column is dropped, a v1 service rollback will fail. Only run Phase 3 after a stabilization window of 7-14 days.

-- Phase 3: Contract -- drop old embedding column
-- Only run after: all rows backfilled, all reads switched to v2,
-- old service version is no longer deployed anywhere
ALTER TABLE document_embeddings
  DROP COLUMN embedding_v1,
  DROP COLUMN embedding_model_v1;

DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF EXISTS idx_embedding_v1;

Dual-write is used when migrating from one storage system to another (e.g., from Pinecone to Weaviate). The application writes to both stores simultaneously during the migration window, then cuts reads to the new store after verifying consistency. Rollback is simple: flip the read flag back to the old store, which has been receiving all writes throughout the migration.

Changing the embedding model requires re-embedding all documents. Old and new embeddings live in different vector spaces -- cosine similarity between a query embedded with Model A and documents embedded with Model B is meaningless. The correct approach: create a new index for the new model, run the backfill into the new index while the old index serves all queries, then cut over using an environment variable. Rollback is re-pointing queries to the old index, which was never modified.

Conversation history stored in a database often includes a structured representation of the prompt. If a migration changes this schema, old service code may fail to deserialize rows written by the new schema. The safe pattern: store the raw JSON as a single JSONB column alongside any derived columns. The raw column is immutable; derived columns are updated by migration. A service rollback always falls back to parsing the raw column.

-- Safe prompt history schema: raw + derived columns
CREATE TABLE conversation_turns (
    id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    session_id  UUID NOT NULL,
    turn_index  INTEGER NOT NULL,
    role        VARCHAR(16) NOT NULL,
    -- Raw: immutable, written once, never migrated
    raw_content JSONB NOT NULL,
    -- Derived: may be updated by migrations; old code ignores unknown fields
    content     TEXT,
    token_count INTEGER,
    model_id    VARCHAR(64),
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

Evaluation results are cached by (prompt_hash, model_id, eval_case_id). If a migration changes the prompt template or the model, the cached eval results are stale. Before any migration that changes a prompt or model, explicitly invalidate the eval cache for the affected (prompt_version, model_id) combinations.

# scripts/invalidate_eval_cache.py
import redis, argparse

def invalidate(r: redis.Redis, prompt_version: str, model_id: str) -> int:
    pattern = f'eval:cache:{prompt_version}:{model_id}:*'
    keys = list(r.scan_iter(pattern))
    if keys:
        r.delete(*keys)
    return len(keys)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument('--prompt-version', required=True)
    parser.add_argument('--model-id', required=True)
    args = parser.parse_args()
    r = redis.Redis.from_url('redis://cache.internal:6379/0')
    n = invalidate(r, args.prompt_version, args.model_id)
    print(f'Invalidated {n} eval cache entries')

When a migration cannot be made backward-compatible, take a point-in-time snapshot before the migration and restore from it if rollback is needed. This is the most reliable rollback path but also the most expensive: restore takes minutes to hours, and any data written between the snapshot and the rollback trigger is lost. For a snapshot restore rollback to be acceptable, the data written in that window must be recoverable from another source (event queue, audit log) or the loss must be explicitly accepted.

# Take a snapshot before a destructive migration (PostgreSQL RDS)
aws rds create-db-snapshot \
  --db-instance-identifier genai-prod \
  --db-snapshot-identifier genai-prod-pre-migration-$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M)

# Wait until snapshot is available before proceeding
aws rds wait db-snapshot-available \
  --db-snapshot-identifier genai-prod-pre-migration-$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M)
echo 'Snapshot ready -- safe to proceed'

# Rollback: restore from snapshot
# WARNING: data written after the snapshot will be lost
aws rds restore-db-instance-from-db-snapshot \
  --db-instance-identifier genai-prod-restored \
  --db-snapshot-identifier genai-prod-pre-migration-<timestamp>

After any data migration rollback, a verification script must confirm that the database is in the expected pre-migration state. This is distinct from the service health check -- the service may be returning HTTP 200 while still reading from an inconsistent intermediate state.

The verification checks depend on the migration pattern. For an expand/contract rollback, confirm that the new column no longer exists (or that all rows have NULL in it if the column was not dropped). For a dual-write rollback, confirm the read flag points to the old store and that a sample query returns the expected results. For a snapshot restore, confirm the row count and a sample of recent rows match the expected pre-migration values.

# scripts/verify_migration_rollback.sh
# Run after any data migration rollback to confirm the stable state
#!/bin/bash
set -e

echo '=== Migration Rollback Verification ==='

# 1. Confirm no rows have NULL in the critical column
psql $DATABASE_URL -c "
  SELECT COUNT(*) AS rows_with_null_embedding
  FROM document_embeddings
  WHERE embedding_v1 IS NULL;
"  # should be 0 after rollback to v1

# 2. Confirm new column no longer exists (if Phase 3 was not reached)
psql $DATABASE_URL -c "
  SELECT column_name FROM information_schema.columns
  WHERE table_name = 'document_embeddings'
    AND column_name = 'embedding_v2';
"  # should return 0 rows if column was dropped on rollback

# 3. Spot-check query results using old embedding
python3 scripts/spot_retrieval_check.py \
  --model ada-002 \
  --index documents-ada-002 \
  --queries test_data/retrieval_gold.jsonl \
  --expected-top-1-hit-rate 0.85

echo 'Verification complete'

Every data migration rollback must be logged with the migration ID, the reason for rollback, the number of rows affected before rollback, and whether any data loss occurred. This log is the primary input for the post-mortem and for determining whether the migration needs a redesign before being retried.

Logging also serves a practical purpose: the next attempt to run the migration needs to know whether the rollback was clean (Phase 1 rollback -- no data affected) or partial (Phase 2 mid-backfill rollback -- some rows have the new column populated). A partial rollback means Phase 2 can be restarted where it left off rather than starting from scratch.

# app/migrations/migration_incident_tracker.py
import dataclasses, datetime, json, pathlib

@dataclasses.dataclass
class MigrationIncident:
    migration_id: str
    started_at: datetime.datetime
    rollback_triggered_at: datetime.datetime | None = None
    rollback_reason: str = ''
    rollback_completed_at: datetime.datetime | None = None
    rows_affected_before_rollback: int = 0
    data_loss: bool = False

def log_rollback(migration_id: str, reason: str, rows_affected: int) -> None:
    incident = MigrationIncident(
        migration_id=migration_id,
        started_at=datetime.datetime.utcnow(),
        rollback_triggered_at=datetime.datetime.utcnow(),
        rollback_reason=reason,
        rows_affected_before_rollback=rows_affected,
    )
    log_dir = pathlib.Path('logs/migration_incidents')
    log_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    path = log_dir / f'{migration_id}_rollback.json'
    with open(path, 'w') as f:
        json.dump(dataclasses.asdict(incident), f, default=str, indent=2)
    print(f'Rollback incident logged: {path}')
Phase 1 rollback (column added, not yet backfilled): re-run is identical to the original -- no state to account for.
Phase 2 partial rollback (backfill in progress): record the last processed row ID so the backfill can resume from that point.
Snapshot restore rollback: record the timestamp of the snapshot used and the approximate number of rows written in the lost window.
Dual-write rollback: record whether the new store had diverged from the old store at the time of rollback, and by how many rows.

Data migration rollback plans must be written for every migration that touches a schema, vector index, prompt history format, or eval cache. The expand/contract pattern is the safest approach: Phase 1 (add nullable columns) is rollbackable in seconds; Phase 2 (backfill with dual-write) is rollbackable without data loss; Phase 3 (drop old columns) is expensive to reverse and requires a stabilization window. Vector index re-embedding uses a parallel index pattern so the old index is never modified. Prompt history schemas are safest when raw JSON is stored alongside derived columns. Snapshot+restore is the escape hatch for non-backward-compatible migrations, with explicit data loss acceptance before proceeding.
